title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
authors: Edward J. Hu, Yelong Shen, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu et al.
hook: Fine-tune giant language models using just a tiny fraction of their weights.
promise: You will understand how LoRA makes adapting large models cheaper without sacrificing performance.
prerequisite_note: If you know why neural networks have millions of weights, you have enough background.

glossary.plain: Tweaking a pre-trained model’s weights to fit a new task or domain.
A way to describe a matrix that can be expressed using fewer numbers than its size suggests.
A small, extra module added to a model for task adaptation, usually increasing inference cost.
A neural network architecture used for language models like GPT and BERT.
Breaking a large matrix into the product of two smaller ones, capturing its essential patterns.
The weights in a model that can be updated during training.

cards.title: Fine-tuning, Shrunk
body: Discover how LoRA slashes the cost of adapting giant language models by learning only what's necessary.

cards.title: Why fine-tuning big models is a headache
body: Modern language models are huge, with billions of weights. Adapting them to new tasks usually means updating all those weights.
But retraining every single parameter is expensive and slow. What if you could adapt a model using only a tiny, focused update?

cards.title: From full fine-tuning to LoRA's shortcut
body: Full fine-tuning changes every weight in a giant model, using lots of memory and compute.
LoRA keeps the original model frozen and learns a small, low-rank change for each layer instead.
This means you get a model adapted to your task, but with far fewer parameters changed or stored.
cards.caption: LoRA swaps expensive full-model updates for small, targeted changes.

cards.title: Fine-tuning: all weights or just a few?
body: Imagine adapting a giant language model for a specific task.
cards.offNote: All weights are updated, using massive memory and storage.
cards.onNote: Only small low-rank matrices are trained, saving resources.

cards.title: The low-rank trick
body: LoRA asks: which parts of a huge model really need to change for a new task? Often, the required changes are much simpler than the full weight matrices.
Instead of updating the whole matrix, LoRA adds a shortcut: it learns two small matrices whose product approximates the needed update. This keeps most of the model frozen.

cards.title: How much of the model do you update?
body: Slide to see what happens as you adjust the rank of LoRA's update.
cards.low: Almost no memory used, but adaptation may be weak.
cards.mid: Good balance: strong adaptation, low cost.
cards.high: Like full fine-tuning: great adaptation, but very expensive.

cards.title: What goes into LoRA?
body: Pick the six essential pieces that make LoRA work.

choices.label: Frozen pre-trained weights
choices.note: Keeps most of the model unchanged.

choices.label: Low-rank matrices
choices.note: Small, trainable updates injected into each layer.

choices.label: Linear projections
choices.note: The parts of the model LoRA modifies.

choices.label: PyTorch integration
choices.note: Lets you use LoRA easily in real models.

cards.title: LoRA's impact: less memory, same results
body: LoRA lets you adapt huge models like GPT-3 using only a fraction of the resources. It matches or beats full fine-tuning on many tasks.
Because LoRA keeps the original model frozen, you can store many different adaptations without needing a copy of the whole model each time.

bars.label: Performance (accuracy)
bars.note: Same or better than full fine-tuning

cards.caption: LoRA keeps adaptation efficient without harming accuracy.

cards.title: Why LoRA is a milestone
body: LoRA made it practical to fine-tune massive models for many users and tasks, without duplicating the entire model each time.
Its low-rank approach inspired a wave of efficient adaptation methods, reshaping how researchers and companies deploy large language models.

recap.label: Freeze
recap.note: Keep the original model's weights unchanged.

recap.label: Inject
recap.note: Add small low-rank matrices into certain layers.

recap.label: Adapt
recap.note: Train only the new, efficient parameters.

recap.label: Deploy
recap.note: Use the same base model for many tasks, saving resources.

cards.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
body: Read the original paper to see the full details, results, and PyTorch code.
